title: Numbers 23
subtitle: The First and Second Oracles of Balaam

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — The First Oracle: A People Who Cannot Be Cursed

heb.gloss: how can I curse whom God has not cursed?
heb.paragraph: mah eqqob lo qabbah El = How can I curse whom God has not cursed?

heb.gloss: a people who dwell alone / who live apart
heb.paragraph: Israel’s distinctive characteristic in the first oracle — they are “a people who live apart and do not consider themselves one of the nations.” The apartness is covenant isolation: not cultural arrogance but divine designation.

refs.note: "I will make your offspring like the dust of the earth, so that if anyone could count the dust, then your offspring could be counted." Balaam’s "who can count the dust of Jacob?" (v.10) directly quotes the Abrahamic promise — the blessing Balak paid to reverse is the promise God made to Abraham that cannot be reversed.

"If God is for us, who can be against us?" The theological logic of the first oracle: God’s prior blessing renders all human opposition ineffective. Paul’s rhetorical question is the NT distillation of Balaam’s reluctant proclamation.

notes.note: MacArthur: The first oracle establishes the foundational principle of the entire Balaam cycle: God’s blessing precedes and overrides human cursing. Balak paid for the reversal of a divine decree — which is theologically impossible regardless of the prophet’s fee. The oracle teaches that the covenant people’s security rests not on their own strength but on the prior word of God that established their blessing.

MacArthur: Seven altars, seven bulls, seven rams — Balaam uses the complete numbers of ANE sacrifice. But God does not need to be persuaded by quantity. He intercepts Balaam and puts his own word in the prophet's mouth. The elaborate ritual setup is irrelevant; what matters is what God chooses to say. No amount of religious performance can manipulate the divine will.

MacArthur: "How can I curse whom God has not cursed?" Balaam's first oracle establishes the foundational truth: Israel's status depends on God's decision, not on human curses or blessings. A people whom God has declared blessed cannot be undone by any prophet, king, or army. This is the doctrine of divine election stated in the mouth of a pagan seer.

MacArthur: "Who can count the dust of Jacob?" — echoing God's promise to Abraham (Gen 13:16). Balaam, hired to diminish Israel, ends up confirming the Abrahamic covenant. "Let me die the death of the upright" reveals that even this mercenary prophet can see the desirability of Israel's destiny, even though he cannot share in it.

Calvin: The first oracle demonstrates that no prayer, ritual, or money can reverse what God has established. Balak assembles every instrument of ancient Near Eastern religious power — altars, sacrifices, a renowned prophet — and produces a blessing. The lesson for the church: its enemies’ most concentrated efforts against God’s people cannot undo what God has spoken over them.

Calvin: The passage on Balaam's oracles demonstrates that God's commands are never arbitrary but expressions of his character applied to Israel's specific circumstances. What he requires, he also enables — the law reveals both the divine standard and the need for grace.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: "Who can count the dust of Jacob" — the phrase echoes Gen 13:16 ("your offspring like the dust of the earth") and Gen 28:14 ("your descendants will be like the dust of the earth"). Balaam’s oracle is saturated with Abrahamic covenant language, confirming that Israel’s blessing is rooted in the patriarchal promises that precede and ground the Sinaitic covenant.

NET Note: The Hebrew vocabulary in this section on Balaam's oracles employs distinctive covenantal terminology. The syntactical construction reinforces the formal, legal register appropriate to the priestly and Deuteronomic tradition.

milgrom.source: Jacob Milgrom, Leviticus 1–6 / 17–22 / 23–27, Anchor Bible (1991–2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Milgrom: "A people who live apart and do not consider themselves one of the nations" — Israel’s apartness (bādad) is not isolation as weakness but isolation as distinction. The covenant community is not like the nations because it is constituted differently — by divine election, not by ethnicity or territory alone. Balaam sees this from the outside and reports it accurately.

Milgrom: The ritual prescriptions for Balaam's oracles reflect a coherent priestly symbolic system. The gradations of holiness and offering type form an internally consistent theology of sacred space and divine access.

ashley.source: Timothy R. Ashley, The Book of Numbers, NICOT (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Ashley: "Let me die the death of the righteous, and may my final end be like theirs!" — Balaam’s personal response to what he sees is astonishing. He wishes to participate in Israel’s destiny. The professional curser is converted by his own oracle into an admirer of the people he was hired to destroy. The divine word works on the speaker as well as the audience.

Ashley: The narrative context of Balaam's oracles in Numbers illustrates the tension between divine provision and human complaint. Each episode advances the theological argument that faithlessness delays but cannot ultimately defeat God's purposes.

hist.context: The first oracle (vv.7–10) is delivered from Bamoth Baal after Balaam encounters God (v.4) and receives the divine word. The oracle has four rhetorical moves: (1) Balak summoned me, but God has not cursed Israel — I cannot (vv.7–8); (2) Israel is a people apart, uncountable in their blessing (v.9); (3) who can count the dust of Jacob? — the Abrahamic promise (v.10a); (4) let me die the death of the righteous (v.10b). Balak’s furious response — "What have you done to me? I brought you here to curse my enemies, but you have done nothing but bless them!" — sets up the second oracle.

sections.header: Verses 13–30 — The Second Oracle: God Is Not a Man Who Lies

heb.gloss: God is not a man that he should lie
heb.paragraph: lo ish El viykhazzev = God is not a man that he should lie

refs.note: "God wanted to make the unchanging nature of his purpose very clear to the heirs of what was promised, he confirmed it with an oath. God did this so that, by two unchangeable things in which it is impossible for God to lie, we who have fled to take hold of the hope set before us may be greatly encouraged." The immutability of God’s covenant word — the second oracle’s central claim — is the Hebrews’ ground for assurance.

"He who is the Glory of Israel does not lie or change his mind; for he is not a human being, that he should change his mind." Samuel quotes the second oracle’s theology directly when addressing Saul’s rejection.

notes.note: MacArthur: "God is not human, that he should lie, not a human being, that he should change his mind. Does he speak and then not act? Does he promise and not fulfil?" — the second oracle’s most important verses. The divine immutability that Balak hoped would bend (perhaps God could be persuaded to reconsider) is precisely what cannot bend. The blessing is irrevocable because the character behind it is unchanging.

MacArthur: Balak moves Balaam to a second vantage point, hoping a different location will produce a different oracle. This is magical thinking — the belief that spiritual outcomes depend on physical positioning. But God's word is not geographically determined. The same blessing will come from any hilltop because its source is heaven, not the hillside.

MacArthur: "God is not man, that he should lie, or a son of man, that he should change his mind." This is one of Scripture's most important theological statements — and it comes from Balaam. God's purposes are irreversible. When he blesses, no curse can overturn it. When he speaks, it stands. Human fickleness is not projected onto the divine character.

MacArthur: "The LORD their God is with them, and the shout of a king is among them." Balaam sees what Israel often cannot: God is present with his people as their warrior-king. The "shout of a king" is the battle cry — God fights for Israel. The oracle concludes with the image of a lion rising from prey. No enchantment works against such a people.

Calvin: "God is not a man that he should lie" is one of the OT’s clearest statements of divine immutability and veracity. The assurance of salvation rests precisely on this: God’s promises cannot be revoked because God’s character cannot change. Balaam’s reluctant proclamation becomes one of the OT’s most comforting theological affirmations.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: "The Lord their God is with them; the shout of the King is among them" — the "shout of the King" (tĕrûʿat melek) refers to the battle cry or acclamation of a divine king present with his army. The same root (tĕrûʿāh) is used for the Feast of Trumpets (Num 29:1) and the ark-cry of Num 10:35. The covenant community’s military victories are grounded in the presence of the divine king.

milgrom.source: Jacob Milgrom, Leviticus 1–6 / 17–22 / 23–27, Anchor Bible (1991–2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Milgrom: The contrast between divine and human speech is the second oracle’s theological core: human words can be retracted; divine words cannot. Balak’s entire enterprise rests on the assumption that prophetic speech can override divine speech — that Balaam’s curse can neutralise God’s blessing. The second oracle demolishes that assumption philosophically before demolishing it practically.

ashley.source: Timothy R. Ashley, The Book of Numbers, NICOT (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Ashley: "No misfortune is seen in Jacob, no misery observed in Israel; the Lord their God is with them" — the second oracle’s pastoral centre. God sees no disqualifying condition in Israel. From Balak’s perspective, Israel is a terrifying military threat; from God’s perspective, Israel is his covenant people in whom no curse-worthy condition exists.

hist.context: Balak moves Balaam to a different vantage point (Zophim on Pisgah — from where he can see only part of Israel) in hopes that a different view will produce a different result. The second oracle (vv.18–24) is more expansive than the first. Its structure: (1) God is not a man who lies or changes his mind; (2) I was told to bless, and I cannot reverse it; (3) God has seen no iniquity in Jacob that would disqualify the blessing; (4) God who brought Israel out of Egypt is like a wild ox in their midst; (5) no sorcery or divination can work against Jacob — God himself is their announcement. Balak’s response: "At least don’t curse them at all." He has retreated from asking for a curse to asking for no blessing.

ppl.name: Balaam↗ People
ppl.role: Mesopotamian seer hired to curse Israel
ppl.text: Balaam son of Beor is attested outside the Bible — the Deir Alla inscription (c.800 BC) names him as a seer from the region. In Num 22–24 he is hired by Balak to curse Israel but God compels him to bless instead, producing four increasingly exalted oracles including the messianic "star from Jacob" (Num 24:17). His donkey sees what he cannot; his death in Num 31:8 confirms his role in the Baal Peor seduction (Num 31:16; Rev 2:14).

ppl.name: Balak↗ People
ppl.role: King of Moab who hired Balaam to curse Israel
ppl.text: Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, is terrified by Israel’s military victories (Num 22:2–4) and commissions Balaam at great expense to produce a prophetic curse. He prepares seven altars and seven sacrifices three times at three different vantage points, each time receiving a blessing instead. His furious dismissal of Balaam without payment (Num 24:10–11) is the oracle cycle’s comic climax. His strategy of seduction through Balaam’s counsel (Num 31:16) succeeds where his direct curse attempt failed.

src.title: Mari Texts and Census Records (c.1800 BC)
src.quote: Administrative texts from Mari record comparable tribal musters, census-taking procedures, and military organisation strikingly similar to Numbers 1–4.
src.note: Confirms that the Numbers census methodology reflects genuine Bronze Age administrative practice rather than later literary invention.

src.title: Balaam Texts from Deir Alla (c.800 BC)
src.quote: A plaster inscription found at Deir Alla in Jordan describes a seer named "Balaam son of Beor" who receives divine night visions — the same figure as Numbers 22–24.
src.note: The extra-biblical attestation of Balaam confirms him as a historical figure known in ancient Transjordanian tradition, lending credibility to the Numbers narrative.

src.title: Ugaritic Priestly Archive Texts
src.quote: Sanctuary service rosters and offering procedures from Ugarit illuminate the social organisation of ancient Near Eastern priesthoods, paralleling the Levitical organisation of Num 3–4.
src.note: The Levitical clan structure and service rotations reflect authentic Bronze Age priestly administrative structures known across the ancient Near East.

rec.text: "Origen’s homilies treat the wilderness journey as a spiritual allegory for the soul’s journey toward God, reading each campsite as a stage in spiritual formation."

hebtext.gloss: how can I curse whom God has not cursed?
hebtext.note: The rhetorical impossibility at the heart of the first oracle: Balaam cannot curse what God has not cursed; cannot denounce what God has not denounced. The curse Balak purchased is theologically impossible — God’s prior blessing renders any human curse void.

hebtext.gloss: a people who dwell alone / who live apart
hebtext.note: Israel’s distinctive characteristic in the first oracle — they are “a people who live apart and do not consider themselves one of the nations.” The apartness is covenant isolation: not cultural arrogance but divine designation.

hebtext.gloss: God is not a man that he should lie
hebtext.note: The second oracle’s theological centre: God’s word is not like human speech — it cannot be retracted, revised, or reversed. What God has spoken over Israel is irrevocable because God’s character is immutable. The oracle is a declaration of divine immutability in the context of prophetic compulsion.

thread.anchor: The First and Second Oracles of Balaam
thread.text: 1 Samuel 15:29 echoes similar theological themes about God's unchangeable nature as found in Balaam's oracles, but it is not a direct quotation.

tx.title: LXX expansion and MT alignment
tx.content: MTandLXXagree closely in Numbers narrative. The LXX occasionally adds explanatory glosses, particularly in the census and itinerary sections.
tx.note: The Qumran Numbers scroll (4QNumb) is the most important textual witness, confirming MT in most passages while occasionally aligning with LXX against MT in minor details.

tx.title: Samaritan Pentateuch and Qumran
tx.content: SPNumbers preserves significant expansions at several points (notably Num 21, 27) that align with Deuteronomy — harmonistic editing for worship at Gerizim.
tx.note: The Dead Sea Scrolls (4QNumb) represent a text-type distinct from both MT and SP, confirming the diversity of the Numbers textual tradition in the Second Temple period.

debate.title: Historical and Literary Integrity of The First and Second Oracles of Balaam

positions.name: Critical scholarship (Noth, Gray)
positions.proponents: Identifies The First and Second Oracles of Balaam as a composite of J, E, and P sources redacted together, with inconsistencies explained by source combination.
positions.argument: The source-critical approach accounts for apparent repetitions and tensions but has been questioned for its circular methodology.

positions.name: Literary and canonical unity (Milgrom, Ashley)
positions.proponents: Reads The First and Second Oracles of Balaam as a literary unit with deliberate structure, arguing that apparent inconsistencies are rhetorical or theological rather than redactional.
positions.argument: The literary approach has gained ground; the chapter’s internal coherence supports reading it as a unified composition.

themes.note: The First and Second Oracles of Balaam

coaching.tip: Balaam's first oracle: 'How can I curse whom God has not cursed?' (v. 8). Hired to curse, compelled to bless. The pagan prophet articulated Israel's election more clearly than many Israelites grasped. Outsiders sometimes see clearer.

Balak moved Balaam to three different vantage points, hoping geography would change the oracle (vv. 13, 27). If you can't change the message, change the view. The strategy assumes that perspective determines prophecy. But God's word isn't contingent on where the prophet stands — it originates from outside the visible landscape entirely.

'God is not human, that he should lie' (v. 19). Balaam's oracles confessed divine faithfulness: what God blesses stays blessed. Balak's money couldn't purchase reversal. Prophetic integrity was forced by divine constraint.

'God is not human, that he should lie, nor a human being, that he should change his mind' (v. 19). This declaration — from a pagan prophet hired to manipulate divine will — is one of the Torah's strongest statements of divine immutability. The theological high point of Numbers comes not from Moses but from an outsider compelled to speak against his own financial interest.

prayer_prompt: Balaam's oracles proclaimed Israel's blessing despite Balak's intentions. Consider asking God how His purposes might be working even through those who oppose you.
